texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation and puri fi cation of carbon monoxide (CO) is of great importance since not only it is a key raw material in the synthesis routes of a variety of basic chemicals, e.g. methanol, formaldehyde, pesticides and herbicides, but also it can be used as fuel if the CO concentration is high enough [1]. Furthermore, it provides relatively inexpensive hydrogen (H2) via water gas shift reaction and represents a feedstock of light hydrocarbons fuels in the Fischer -Tropsch process. However, the growth of CO as starting material relies upon the availability of relative inexpensive sources of CO [2]. In this sense, abundant and low-cost CO is very often released together with nitrogen (N2) in the off gas streams from reductive combustion processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, facilitated transport supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs) were prepared with the room temperature ionic liquid (RTIL) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([hmim][Cl]) and the cuprous chloride (CuCl) copper(I) salt to perform the selective separation of CO from N2. Previously, we reported that this solute-carrier system provides large CO/N2 solubility selectivity due to the reversible formation of a CO -copper(I) complex. The RTIL was impregnated in a porous PVDF membrane and pure and mixed gas permeation experiments were performed with continuous gas fl ow at constant pressure. The effects of different operating conditions were tested, namely pressure, temperature, sweep gas fl ow rate and liquid membrane composition. The CO permeability through the copper(I)-based SILMs increases with respect to pure [hmim][Cl] -SILMs owing to the CO carrier-mediated transport. On the contrary, the N2 fl ux is not affected, and therefore, the CO/N2 selectivity is increased. The CO transport through the reactive SILMs is enhanced at higher temperatures and copper(I) concentration. However, mixed gas experiments revealed a slight decrease of CO/N2 selectivity. Regarding membrane stability, SILMs were continuously operated for two weeks without any loss of performance. Additionally, gas diffusion coef fi cients (~10 -10 m 2 s -1 ) and solute-carrier complex diffusivity (~10 -11 m 2 s -1 ) were calculated in the RTIL media.

Previously, we reported that this solute-carrier system provides large CO/N2 solubility selectivity due to the reversible formation of a CO -copper(I) complex. The RTIL was impregnated in a porous PVDF membrane and pure and mixed gas permeation experiments were performed with continuous gas fl ow at constant pressure. The effects of different operating conditions were tested, namely pressure, temperature, sweep gas fl ow rate and liquid membrane composition. The CO permeability through the copper(I)-based SILMs increases with respect to pure [hmim][Cl] -SILMs owing to the CO carrier-mediated transport. On the contrary, the N2 fl ux is not affected, and therefore, the CO/N2 selectivity is increased. The CO transport through the reactive SILMs is enhanced at higher temperatures and copper(I) concentration. However, mixed gas experiments revealed a slight decrease of CO/N2 selectivity. Regarding membrane stability, SILMs were continuously operated for two weeks without any loss of performance. Additionally, gas diffusion coef fi cients (~10 -10 m 2 s -1 ) and solute-carrier complex diffusivity (~10 -11 m 2 s -1 ) were calculated in the RTIL media.

texts.orig: COSORB process, are rarely employed owing to several concerns, namely long-term activity and stability of the absorbent and its relatively high energy and operational costs. In addition, pressure swing adsorption (PSA) is also an energy consuming process that needs expensive equipments and has inherent variations in pressure and composition [5]. On the other hand, membrane technology is seen as a promising alternative to the traditional separation processes because of their effective performance, modularity and reduced energy requirement and operating costs for the same degree of separation achievable [6]. However, the most important membrane applications involving CO separation employ hydrogenpermselective membranes, e.g. metallic membranes [7,8], carbon membranes [9] and polymeric membranes [5,10,11], for either puri fi cation of H2 or H2/CO ratio adjustment usually in methane reforming and syngas plants [12]. On the contrary, the study of CO permeation through membranes is scarce in literature.

texts.orig: However, there are many concerns regarding carrier solubility in common solvents and long-term performance of SLMs since traditional organic and aqueous solvents can evaporate or leak from the supporting membrane [18]. Several approaches have been proposed to overcome the instability problems such as gelation or top layer formation by interfacial polymerization, but in the last two decades one of the most promising alternatives to improve the membrane stability has been the replacement of the conventional volatile solvents by room temperature ionic liquids (RTILs). RTILs are liquid salts at ambient temperature, entirely composed of highly asymmetric organic cations and organic or inorganic anions, which have unique properties regarding their use in the so-called supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs), i.e. negligible vapour pressure, high surface tension, high thermal stability, excellent solvent properties and task-speci fi city [19]. In the fi eld of gas separation, SILMs have been widely studied for CO2 capture owing to its enhanced solubility compared to that of other gases [20], mainly N2 and CH4, but also for SO2 recovery [21] and ole fi n/paraf fi n separation [22].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrophilic polylvinylidene fl uoride (PVDF) microporous membranes (FP-Vericel, 0.2 μ m pore size, 70% porosity, Pall Corporation) were selected as support material of the SILMs to assure a good wettability since [hmim][Cl] is a highly hydrophilic RTIL. The pore size of the membrane, the surface tension of the RTILs, higher than that of traditional organic solvents [28], as well as membrane wettability, are key factors affecting SILM stability [29]. In this work, the maximum transmembrane pressure that can be applied across the SILM in order to avoid the displacement of the RTIL from the pores was estimated applying the Young -Laplace equation to be approximately 300 kPa at 303 K. A contact angle of 50 1 was assumed in the calculation based on values reported for other RTILs with similar surface tension to [hmim][Cl] on PVDF supports [30]. However, since that equation is valid for ideal cylindrical pores with a narrow pore size distribution, this is a rough estimation and therefore, the real critical pressure is usually lower. The PVDF membrane thickness was determined using a digital micrometer (Mitutoyo 369 -250, 7 0.001 mm accuracy), being the average value 129 μ m.

texts.orig: 2.2. SILM preparation
texts.text: 2.2. SILM preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prepare the SILMs, fi rst of all, [hmim][Cl] was heated at 343 K for 1 -2 h while applying vacuum ( o 2 mbar) in order to remove water and any other traces of volatile compounds before its impregnation on the porous support. In the case of preparing a reactive SILM, CuCl was added to the RTIL and the solution was stirred at 343 K in an inert nitrogen atmosphere so as to avoid copper(I) oxidation, hence obtaining a chlorocuprate(I)-based RTIL [25]. Then, the PVDF membrane, cut to a 45 mm diameter disc, was left to soak up the RTIL for 2 h in a vacuum oven thus allowing the removal of air from the membrane pores. After impregnation, the SILM was allowed to drip overnight to remove excess liquid from the surface. In addition, the SILM was placed between two hydrophobic polypropylene membranes (PolySep, 0.22 μ m pore size) to avoid RTIL leakage from the membrane pores. Finally, the SILM assembly was placed in the permeation test cell and a 150 kPa nitrogen stream was applied until a constant nitrogen fl ux across the SILM was reached, typically within 2 -3 h. During this last step, the nitrogen fl ux across the SILM was found to decrease exponentially with time, a behaviour that may be due to the combination of two factors: the complete fi lling of the membrane pores with the RTIL and the removal of moisture from the SILM. After impregnation, the SILM was allowed to drip overnight to remove excess liquid from the surface. In addition, the SILM was placed between two hydrophobic polypropylene membranes (PolySep, 0.22 μ m pore size) to avoid RTIL leakage from the membrane pores. Finally, the SILM assembly was placed in the permeation test cell and a 150 kPa nitrogen stream was applied until a constant nitrogen fl ux across the SILM was reached, typically within 2 -3 h. During this last step, the nitrogen fl ux across the SILM was found to decrease exponentially with time, a behaviour that may be due to the combination of two factors: the complete fi lling of the membrane pores with the RTIL and the removal of moisture from the SILM. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Gas permeation experiments
texts.text: 2.3. Gas permeation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimental set-up employed for gas permeation is depicted in Fig. 1. Basically, the SILM is placed over a metallic porous support inside a self-made stainless steel permeation cell leading to an effective membrane area of approximately 12.5 cm 2 . The con fi guration of the set-up allows feeding the upper chamber with either pure or mixed gas stream by means of individual mass fl ow controllers (Brooks 5850S, fl ow rate: 0 -100 cm 3 (STP) min -1 ). The retentate stream leaves the permeation cell via a backpressure regulator (Brooks 5866), which controls the total pressure in the feed chamber. In the permeate compartment, which is kept at atmospheric pressure, the permeate gas is removed by an argon (Ar) sweep stream (Brooks 5850S, 4 cm 3 (STP) min -1 ) and eventually, the concentration of each gas is determined in a gas chromatograph (Agilent 3000 Micro GC) equipped with two columns: a molecular sieve for CO and N2 and a PLOT U for CO2. In addition, since the cell is thermostated inside a heating oven, experiments can be conducted at different temperatures.

Each experiment was allowed to proceed until the permeate composition remained unchanged for at least 2 h. When the temperature was shifted, fi rst, a N2 stream was applied overnight until a steady permeate fl ux was reached at the desired temperature. Permeation data were obtained through at least two SILMs prepared under the same conditions. The mean absolute error of the replicated experiments did not surpass 7 7% in any case.

texts.orig: The permeate fl uxes of CO and N2 through the pure [hmim] [Cl] -SILMs are plotted in Fig. 2 at 303 K and increasing gas permeation driving force, i.e. the partial pressure difference across the membrane, which was varied from 150 kPa to 250 kPa. The permeate fl uxes show a linear increase with the driving force in good agreement with the fundamentals of a solution-diffusionbased mass transport process [38], indicating at the same time that CO facilitated transport is not taking place through the [hmim][Cl] -SILM [39]. Consequently, the permeability and ideal gas selectivity remain unchanged in that pressure range. This behaviour has already been reported by Fallanza et al. [22] in a similar pressure range, 20 -165 kPa, for the gas pair propylene/ propane using SILMs based in Ag(I) -[bmim][BF4]. The permeability coef fi cients of CO and N2 through the bulk [hmim][Cl] are 11.8 barrers and 6.0 barrers, respectively, and the CO/N2 ideal selectivity has a value of 2.0. texts.orig: The temperature effect on N2 and CO permeability was also studied in the range of temperatures from 303 to 323 K. The permeability increases as temperature increases, varying from 6 barrers to 8.6 barrers for N2, and 11.8 barrers to 16 barrers for CO. This increase in gas permeability indicates that diffusion controls the transfer rate of CO and N2, since the solubilities of both gases in [hmim][Cl] decrease with temperature as reported in our previous work [23]. This trend has also been reported for H2 in a number of ionic liquids by Cserjési et al. [41] and for CO2 and CH4 by Adibi et al. [32] and Barghi et al. [35]. Fig. 3 shows that the natural logarithm of the permeability coef fi cients follows a linear relationship with the inverse of temperature; hence, the temperature dependence of this transport parameter can be described by the Arrhenius -van ' t Hoff equations [42]. The activation energies of permeation, Ep , are 12.4 and 14.7 kJ mol -1 for CO and N2, respectively. These values are consistent with those found by Adibi et al. [32] for CO2 (8 kJ mol -1 ) and CH4 (29.3 kJ mol -1 ) in [hmim][NTf2].

texts.text: 3.4. Gas permeation through copper(I)-containing SILMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to determine whether the carrier-mediated transport of CO takes place through [hmim][Cl] -SILMs containing a copper (I) salt, SILMs were prepared with two different concentrations of CuCl in [hmim][Cl]; the molar ratios of copper salt to ionic liquid used were 0.38 and 0.5, which correspond to 1.6 and 2 M CuCl solutions in [hmim][Cl], respectively. The reaction between CO and ð CuCl Þ 2 -3 described by Eq. (1) takes place at the feed side of the membrane and forms the solute-carrier complex ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3 , while at the permeate side, the low partial pressure of CO shifts the reaction to the left side and therefore, carbon monoxide is released into the sweep gas stream. To assure that the maximum driving force was achieved in these experiments, the Ar sweep gas fl ow rate was initially varied in the range 2 -10 cm 3 (STP) min -1 . The results showed that the CO fl ux across the SILM was unaffected by this variable, hence a fl ow rate of 4 cm 3 (STP) min -1 was selected to perform the experiments.

It can be seen that N2 permeability undergoes a marginal increase from the pure [hmim] [Cl] -SILM to the SILMs containing copper(I) that may be assigned to the decrease in the RTIL viscosity after the CuCl addition. In contrast, after adding the copper(I) salt to the [hmim][Cl], the permeability of CO through the SILM is enhanced as a result of the increased capacity of the medium to solubilise CO owing to the π -complexation reaction between CO and the copper(I)-carrier (Eq. (1)). However, the values of the facilitation factor of CO ð FT CO Þ are lower than those reported by Dindi et al. [15] through traditional supported liquid membranes based on aqueous solutions of copper(I), that changed from 5 to as high as 20 depending on the operating conditions. This difference is most likely due to the lower value of the equilibrium constant of the complexation reaction between CO and copper(I) in the ionic liquid media than that found in conventional aqueous systems.

It can be noticed that the selectivity increases with temperature and copper(I) concentration, although the latter effect is less signi fi cant. Conversely, in the pure ionic liquid SILMs, the selectivity remains almost constant with increasing temperature as it was aforementioned. The small, although noticeable, increase in selectivity between both copper (I)-based SILMs is explained in terms of the low pressure gradient applied, which results in a low solubility improvement when CuCl/ [hmim][Cl] molar ratio is increased from 0.38 to 0.5. Hence, a higher driving force would be needed so as to achieve higher selectivities

texts.orig: The diffusivity coef fi cients of N2 through the pure [hmim][Cl] and through [CuCl]/[hmim][Cl] in the whole range of temperatures were calculated from the experimentally obtained permeability data by applying Eq. (5). The solubility data needed in that calculation were determined in our previous work [23]. The calculated diffusion coef fi cients of N2 in [hmim][Cl] are plotted in Fig. 5 as a function of the viscosity of the RTIL which accounts for temperature variations. Available diffusivity data of CO2 obtained with a time-lag method [32,35,43,44] are also plotted as benchmarks since both gases are expected to have almost identical diffusivities in RTILs according to predictions using theoretical models such as the Morgan ' s predictive model [43]. This model takes into account the liquid molar volume of the gas at its atmospheric boiling point as the only signi fi cant variable of the solute to predict gas diffusivity in RTILs. The molar volumes of CO2 and N2 are 33.3 cm 3 mol -1 and 31.9 cm 3 mol -1 , respectively, according to the Tyn and Calus method [45]. In addition, the diffusivity of gaseous CO, which cannot be experimentally determined in the reactive medium, can be considered analogous to that of N2 and CO2 as its liquid molar volume is 33 cm 3 mol -1 . This ' CO -N2 analogy ' has already been employed and experimentally con fi rmed for the COSORB process [46].

texts.orig: From Fig. 5, it can be seen that N2 diffusivity through the pure [hmim][Cl] calculated from experimental data is much higher than the diffusivity predicted by Morgan ' s equation. On the other hand, the observed N2 diffusivity (and therefore CO diffusivity) through the copper(I)-based RTIL is almost equal to the N2 diffusivity through the pure [hmim][Cl] despite the decrease of the medium viscosity after CuCl addition. Moreover, it is much more consistent with reported values of CO2 diffusivity and Morgan ' s predictive model as shown in Fig. 5. texts.orig: The same methodology was applied to determine the diffusion of the permeate-carrier complex ð D ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3  Þ in the reactive medium. However, in order to use Eq. (5) in this case, the hypothesis that the rate of chemical reaction is fast compared to the rate of diffusion has to be ful fi lled. In this sense, Hogendoorn et al. [46] demonstrated that the reaction between CO and copper(I) can be considered instantaneous with respect to mass transfer in the COSORB process. Hence, it is expected that it will also be valid for our system, even more when the mass transfer is worsened with respect to COSORB process due to the higher viscosity of the RTIL media. Therefore, the rate of transfer of CO is thought to be controlled by the rate of diffusion of the complex ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3 , whereas the normal passive diffusion of gaseous CO is ignored [47]. Table 5 collects the CO solubility parameters in the copper(I)-based RTIL and the calculated ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3 complex diffusivity as a function of temperature and copper(I) concentration. Since the CO solubility in the copper(I) -RTIL depends on pressure, the solubility parameters were calculated as the slopes of the tangents to the absorption isotherms obtained in our previous work [23] at the desired pressure value. This approach is particularly suitable at the low pressure conditions applied in this work.
texts.text: The same methodology was applied to determine the diffusion of the permeate-carrier complex ð D ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3  Þ in the reactive medium. However, in order to use Eq. (5) in this case, the hypothesis that the rate of chemical reaction is fast compared to the rate of diffusion has to be ful fi lled. In this sense, Hogendoorn et al. [46] demonstrated that the reaction between CO and copper(I) can be considered instantaneous with respect to mass transfer in the COSORB process. Hence, it is expected that it will also be valid for our system, even more when the mass transfer is worsened with respect to COSORB process due to the higher viscosity of the RTIL media. Therefore, the rate of transfer of CO is thought to be controlled by the rate of diffusion of the complex ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3 , whereas the normal passive diffusion of gaseous CO is ignored [47]. Table 5 collects the CO solubility parameters in the copper(I)-based RTIL and the calculated ½ Cu ð CO Þ Cl  2 -3 complex diffusivity as a function of temperature and copper(I) concentration. Since the CO solubility in the copper(I) -RTIL depends on pressure, the solubility parameters were calculated as the slopes of the tangents to the absorption isotherms obtained in our previous work [23] at the desired pressure value. This approach is particularly suitable at the low pressure conditions applied in this work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Gas mixture permeation
texts.text: 3.6. Gas mixture permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With respect to process design, it is important to know whether the SILMs present a similar behaviour with pure and mixed feed gas streams. Therefore, mixed gas permeation experiments were performed to determine CO and N2 permeability and CO/N2 selectivity and to compare these results with those previously obtained with pure feed gases. These permeation experiments were carried out with both the SILMs containing the pure ionic liquid and those containing CuCl dissolved at different ratios. The temperature was also varied in all cases from 303 K to 323 K. In Fig. 6, it is shown a comparison between the CO/N2 selectivity calculated from pure gas permeation data and that obtained with a 50/50 vol% mixed gas feed at various temperatures and 0.38 CuCl/[hmim][Cl] molar ratio. The same trend was found in the experiments performed at the other copper(I) concentrations and therefore, the results are not shown. It can be observed that the CO/N2 selectivity suffers a small decrease in all the experiments performed with mixed gas feed with respect to the ideal selectivity. This reduction in CO/N2 selectivity is due to an increase in N2 permeability when mixed gas feeds are employed. On the other hand, no signi fi cant changes are appreciated in CO permeability when the feed consists of a gas mixture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Neves et al. [52] and Scovazzo et al. [53] also found slight differences between ideal and mixed gas selectivities for the gas pairs CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 through SILMs. This trend is much less noticeable than in polymer membranes, in which the mixed gas selectivities may be one-half or less than the calculated value from pure gas measurements if the gases in the mixture strongly interact with the membrane material. This difference between SILMs and polymer membranes is a direct consequence of the key factor that determines membrane selectivity for a given gas pair in each kind of membrane. In the case of SILMs, it is the solubility selectivity that governs the membrane selectivity; on the contrary, diffusivity selectivity is the key factor in strongly size-sieving polymer membranes (glassy membranes) [53]. Therefore, a change in diffusivity selectivity as a consequence of membrane swelling produced by the sorption of condensable components can strongly affect the selectivity of glassy membranes [42] whereas it does not affect SILM selectivity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.7. SILM stability
texts.text: 3.7. SILM stability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding SILMs stability, it was found that when the pressure difference across the SILM was set above 250 kPa a sharp change of the gas fl ux took place within 1 -2 days. Since the fl ux did not return to the original value when the previous pressure conditions were established again, it was concluded that the applied pressure exceeded the burst out pressure of the RTIL, related to the capillary forces within the membrane pores [52]. On the contrary, at 150 kPa, SILMs are far more stable, to the point that they were changed only at the end of the testing period without any noticeable loss of performance. In addition, the same SILM was employed to test different experimental conditions over periods up to two weeks. As an example, Fig. 7 collects the data of all the experiments performed with a copper(I)-based SILM including pure and mixed gas as well as temperature variations. Concerning carrier stability, a partial oxidation of the copper(I)-carrier was not observed since, as it can be appreciated in Fig. 7, the permeability of CO was greatly enhanced even after long operation time. In fact, when the SILMs were removed they kept their original greenish yellow colour, whereas if the copper(I)-based solution is left in contact with air it darkens with time.
texts.text: Regarding SILMs stability, it was found that when the pressure difference across the SILM was set above 250 kPa a sharp change of the gas fl ux took place within 1 -2 days. Since the fl ux did not return to the original value when the previous pressure conditions were established again, it was concluded that the applied pressure exceeded the burst out pressure of the RTIL, related to the capillary forces within the membrane pores [52]. On the contrary, at 150 kPa, SILMs are far more stable, to the point that they were changed only at the end of the testing period without any noticeable loss of performance. In addition, the same SILM was employed to test different experimental conditions over periods up to two weeks. As an example, Fig. 7 collects the data of all the experiments performed with a copper(I)-based SILM including pure and mixed gas as well as temperature variations. Concerning carrier stability, a partial oxidation of the copper(I)-carrier was not observed since, as it can be appreciated in Fig. 7, the permeability of CO was greatly enhanced even after long operation time. In fact, when the SILMs were removed they kept their original greenish yellow colour, whereas if the copper(I)-based solution is left in contact with air it darkens with time.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we take advantage of the high capacity of the system formed by the RTIL [hmim][Cl] and the salt CuCl to solubilise carbon monoxide with the aim of developing longterm stable copper(I)-based SILMs, that overcome the instability problems associated with traditional SLMs, and assess their performance to selectively separate CO from N2. In contrast to polymeric membranes, this separation is feasible employing CuCl/ [hmim][Cl] -SILMs with selectivities that varied in the range 2 -4 depending on the testing conditions. Facilitated transport of CO, and therefore CO/N2 selectivity, increases with temperature and copper(I) carrier concentration; however, mixed gas experiments reveal a slight decrease of ~20% in the selectivity with respect to the ideal selectivity.
texts.text: In this work, we take advantage of the high capacity of the system formed by the RTIL [hmim][Cl] and the salt CuCl to solubilise carbon monoxide with the aim of developing longterm stable copper(I)-based SILMs, that overcome the instability problems associated with traditional SLMs, and assess their performance to selectively separate CO from N2. In contrast to polymeric membranes, this separation is feasible employing CuCl/ [hmim][Cl] -SILMs with selectivities that varied in the range 2 -4 depending on the testing conditions. Facilitated transport of CO, and therefore CO/N2 selectivity, increases with temperature and copper(I) carrier concentration; however, mixed gas experiments reveal a slight decrease of ~20% in the selectivity with respect to the ideal selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the improvement of CO/N2 selectivity after the addition of CuCl to [hmim][Cl], the facilitated transport of CO through these SILMs is lower than the values reported through other carriersolvent systems. The difference is attributed to two factors: (i) the low equilibrium constant of the complexation reaction that provides relatively low facilitated transport fl uxes at the applied low pressure gradients [47], and (ii) the lower diffusivity of the anionic CO -Cu(I) complex in the RTIL media (~10 -11 m 2 s -1 ) when compared to values found for similar species in water or organic solvents (~10 -9 m 2 s -1 ) [15]. However, a thorough kinetic study of the complexation reaction between CO and copper(I) in this RTIL medium is in progress and it will be used in the development of a rigorous mass transfer model of CO permeation through the reactive SILM. In addition, future research concerning both improvements in membrane stability that allow to operate at higher pressure gradients where the carrier pathway is fully utilised, and exploration of new RTIL media with lower viscosities, which enhance the solute-carrier complex diffusivity, is needed in order to consider this membrane separation a realistic alternative to traditional separation processes.
